extra.reasoning:
The property "globally(opted_out implies not sent)" holds on every reachable execution.

Only one transition sets sent := true: [t_send_allowed] on send_attempt, guarded by "not opted_out". So sent can only be set true while opted_out is false.

opted_out is monotonic: the only transition that sets it is [t_optout] (on optout, unconditionally setting opted_out := true), and NO transition ever sets opted_out := false. Once true, it stays true forever.

Crucially, [t_optout] also sets sent := false in the same atomic step. So at the exact moment opted_out becomes true, sent is simultaneously cleared.

In the opted-out region (opted_out == true), can sent ever become true again? The only sender [t_send_allowed] has guard "not opted_out", which is permanently false there, so it can never fire. Every other transition in this region either sets sent := false (t_send_blocked on send_attempt, and the stage transitions adv_0/adv_1/rst_* all set sent := false) or leaves state unchanged (no matching enabled transition). Since sent is already false upon entering the opted-out region, it remains false.

The combination-lock mechanism (stage 0->1 via key_1, 1->2 via key_0, resets via key_0 at stage 0 / key_1/key_2) is irrelevant to this property: reaching any stage never enables a send. There is no send path gated on stage; the sole send path depends only on not opted_out. Moreover every stage transition explicitly sets sent := false. The lock can therefore never produce a post-opt-out send.

Therefore opted_out implies not sent in every reachable state, within 5 events and in general. No counterexample exists.
